content:
# Acts Chapter 23 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 254-255

---

**1** And Paul, looking intently at the council, said, "Brothers, I have lived my life before God in all good conscience up to this day."

**2** And the high priest Ananias commanded those standing beside him to strike him on the mouth.

**3** Then Paul said to him, "God is about to strike you, you whitewashed wall! You sit judging me according to the law, and yet in violation of the law you command me to be struck?"

**4** And those standing by said, "Do you revile the high priest of God?"

**5** And Paul said, "I did not know, brothers, that he is the high priest, for it is written, 'You shall not speak evil of a ruler of your people.'"

**6** Now when Paul perceived that one part were Sadducees and the other Pharisees, he cried out in the council, "Brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees. It is concerning the hope and the resurrection of the dead that I am being judged."

**7** And when he had said this, a dissension arose between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the assembly was divided.

**8** For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit, but the Pharisees acknowledge them all.

**9** And a great clamor arose, and some of the scribes of the Pharisees' party stood up and contended sharply, saying, "We find nothing evil in this man. What if a spirit spoke to him, or an angel--"

**10** And when the dissension became great, the commander, fearing that Paul would be torn apart by them, commanded the soldiers to go down and take him by force from among them and bring him into the barracks.

**11** And the following night the Lord stood by him and said, "Take courage, for as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome."

**12** And when it was day, the Jews made a plot and bound themselves under a curse, saying that they would neither eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.

**13** And there were more than forty who made this conspiracy.

**14** And they went to the chief priests and the elders and said, "We have bound ourselves under a great curse to taste nothing until we have killed Paul.

**15** Now therefore you, together with the council, give notice to the commander to bring him down to you, as though you were going to determine his case more precisely. And we, before he draws near, are ready to kill him."

**16** But the son of Paul's sister heard of the ambush, and he came and entered the barracks and reported it to Paul.

**17** And Paul called one of the centurions and said, "Bring this young man to the commander, for he has something to report to him."

**18** So he took him and brought him to the commander and said, "The prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you, as he has something to say to you."

**19** And the commander took him by the hand, and withdrawing privately, asked, "What is it that you have to report to me?"

**20** And he said, "The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the council tomorrow, as though they were going to inquire more precisely concerning him.

**21** Therefore do not be persuaded by them, for more than forty of their men are lying in ambush for him, who have bound themselves under a curse neither to eat nor to drink until they have killed him. And now they are ready, awaiting the promise from you."

**22** So the commander dismissed the young man, charging him, "Tell no one that you have revealed these things to me."

**23** And he summoned two of the centurions and said, "Prepare two hundred soldiers to go to Caesarea, and seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen, from the third hour of the night.

**24** Also provide mounts to set Paul upon, that they may bring him safely to Felix the governor."

**25** And he wrote a letter having this form:

**26** "Claudius Lysias, to the most excellent governor Felix, greetings.

**27** This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them, when I came upon them with the soldiers and rescued him, having learned that he was a Roman.

**28** And desiring to know the charge for which they were accusing him, I brought him down to their council.

**29** I found him to be accused concerning questions of their law, but having no charge worthy of death or of chains.

**30** And when it was disclosed to me that there would be a plot against the man, I sent him to you at once, also commanding his accusers to state their case against him before you."

**31** So the soldiers, according to their orders, took Paul and brought him by night to Antipatris.

**32** And on the next day, they returned to the barracks, leaving the horsemen to go on with him.

**33** And when they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor, they also presented Paul to him.

**34** And when he had read it, he asked from what province he was. And learning that he was from Cilicia,

**35** he said, "I will give you a full hearing when your accusers also arrive." And he commanded him to be kept in the praetorium of Herod.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 23 spans pages 254-255 of Codex Sinaiticus, covering Paul's appearance before the Sanhedrin, the divine night-vision commissioning him to Rome, the assassination conspiracy, and the military transfer to Caesarea. The chapter pivots the narrative decisively westward: after the Lord's command in verse 11, every subsequent event in Acts drives toward Rome.

Six key issues required adjudication. First, the "whitewashed wall" (TOICHE KEKONIAMENE) in v. 3 is carried unanimously by all three translators. The perfect passive participle ("having been whitewashed") connects to Ezekiel 13:10-15 and the prophetic tradition of concealed corruption. Paul's insult is unusually sharp for an apostle addressing the high priest, and his partial retraction in v. 5 has generated centuries of debate.

Second, "I did not know" (OUK EDEIN) in v. 5 presents a genuine interpretive crux. Whether Paul genuinely failed to recognize Ananias, spoke with deliberate irony, or offered a formal apology regardless of recognition, the text does not resolve. SOPHIA renders the pluperfect straightforwardly as "I did not know" -- matching LOGOS and PNEUMA -- preserving the ambiguity without forcing either reading. EMET's "I had not known" captures the pluperfect tense more precisely but sounds unnatural in English; the simple past "did not know" adequately conveys the past-state meaning of EDEIN.

Third, Sinaiticus reads HUIOS PHARISAION ("son of Pharisees," plural) in v. 6, not the singular PHARISAIOU found in some Byzantine manuscripts. All three translators follow Sinaiticus. PNEUMA renders "son of a Pharisee" (singular), departing from the manuscript; SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with the plural "son of Pharisees," which emphasizes Paul's multi-generational Pharisaic lineage.

Fourth, in v. 8, Sinaiticus reads AMPHOTERA ("both") for the Pharisaic affirmation, though three items are denied by the Sadducees (resurrection, angel, spirit). The "both" likely treats angel and spirit as a single category of spiritual beings. EMET renders "both" literally; LOGOS renders "them all" to smooth the apparent inconsistency; PNEUMA renders "all of these." SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "them all" -- the idiomatic sense of AMPHOTERA here encompasses all three items.

Fifth, in v. 9, Sinaiticus preserves the shorter reading, omitting "let us not fight against God" (ME THEOMACHOMEN) found in Byzantine witnesses -- likely a scribal addition influenced by Gamaliel's speech in Acts 5:39. The sentence trails off in aposiopesis ("What if a spirit spoke to him, or an angel--"), which all three translators preserve. SOPHIA renders the trailing break with an em-dash.

Sixth, DEXIOLABOUS in v. 23 is a hapax legomenon whose precise meaning remains debated: possibly light-armed spearmen, javelin-throwers, or bodyguards ("those who seize with the right hand"). All three translators render "spearmen," which SOPHIA adopts as the standard convention while noting the uncertainty.

Lysias's letter (vv. 26-30) contains a deliberate distortion: the commander claims he rescued Paul "having learned that he was a Roman" (v. 27), when in fact he discovered Paul's citizenship only after ordering him bound for flogging (22:25-29). Luke preserves this self-serving narrative without editorial correction, trusting the reader to recall the actual sequence. SOPHIA translates the letter as written.

All disagreements are logged in the separate disagreement file.

---
